title: Sliding Window Minimum
description:
You are given an array of n integers generated by a recurrence:
- x1 = x
- xi = (a·x_{i−1} + b) mod c for i = 2..n

Given a window size k, for each contiguous window of k elements from left to right, compute its minimum. Output the XOR of all these window minimums.

Input:
- First line: n k
- Second line: x a b c

Output:
- A single integer: the XOR of all window minimums.

Note: n can be as large as 1e7. The array must be generated on the fly.

examples.explanation: Generated sequence: [3,0,1,8,2,4,7,6]. Window minima for k=5: [0,0,1,2]. XOR: 0^0^1^2 = 3.

mcqChain.questionText: Using the generator rules with x=3, a=7, b=1, c=11, what are the first 5 elements x1..x5?

wrongExplanations.1: You cannot use raw inputs as sequence; values are generated by the recurrence.
wrongExplanations.3: Modulo 11 results do not match those values; recompute each step carefully.

mcqChain.hint: Compute step by step using modulo.

mcqChain.questionText: For array [3,0,1,8,2,4,7,6] and k=5, what are the window minima from left to right?

wrongExplanations.1: The first minimum is 0, not 3.

mcqChain.hint: Find the smallest number in each k-length window.

mcqChain.questionText: If k = 1, what is the output conceptually equal to?

options.label: The XOR of the global minimum repeated n times
The minimum of the entire array

mcqChain.explanation: Each window of size 1 has its only element as the minimum. XOR all elements.

wrongExplanations.1: We are not summing; we combine via XOR.
wrongExplanations.3: With k=1, there are n windows, each min is the element itself.

mcqChain.hint: The minimum of a 1-element window is that element.

mcqChain.questionText: How many window minima must be XORed for array length n and window size k?
mcqChain.explanation: Windows start at positions 1..(n−k+1).

wrongExplanations.1: We slide by 1, not by k.
wrongExplanations.2: Off by one: the last valid start is n−k+1.

mcqChain.hint: First window is [1..k], last starts at n−k+1.

mcqChain.questionText: A naive method scans k elements to find the minimum in each window. What is its time cost and feasibility for n up to 1e7?

options.label: O(n·k), too slow for large n and k
O(n log k), feasible for any n

mcqChain.explanation: Scanning k per window yields O(n·k), not acceptable for worst-case sizes.

wrongExplanations.3: O(n log k) requires a different approach than naive scanning.

mcqChain.hint: How many windows are there and what does each scan cost?

mcqChain.questionText: To update the window minimum efficiently when moving one step right, what should we maintain?

options.label: Indices of candidates in a double-ended structure so that the front is the smallest in the current window
A fully sorted list of all elements seen so far
A running average of the window

mcqChain.explanation: Keep candidate indices in order so the front is the window’s smallest; remove outdated and worse candidates.

wrongExplanations.1: If the minimum leaves the window, you can’t update without more info.
wrongExplanations.2: Sorting all seen elements is unnecessary and too slow.

mcqChain.hint: We need fast removal of old indices and fast access to the smallest.

mcqChain.questionText: When adding a new element v at index i, how should we adjust the back of our structure to keep only useful candidates?

options.label: Remove from the back while the last value is >= v, then append (v,i)
Never remove from the back; just append (v,i)
Remove from the back only if the last value is > v, then append (v,i)
Replace the last element with (v,i) regardless of comparison

mcqChain.explanation: Any element with value ≥ v behind cannot become the minimum before v; remove them to keep candidates small.

wrongExplanations.2: Using > leaves equal duplicates; removing ≥ keeps structure smaller without losing correctness.

mcqChain.hint: Which older elements can never win against the new one?

mcqChain.questionText: When the current window ends at index i (0-based), which front indices are out of the window of size k and must be removed?
mcqChain.explanation: Window starts at i−k+1. Indices ≤ i−k are strictly before the window and must be removed.

wrongExplanations.1: Using < misses the element at i−k which is outside.
wrongExplanations.2: This removes the element at i−k+1 which is still inside.

mcqChain.hint: Front index must be >= i−k+1 to stay.

mcqChain.questionText: Simulate the candidate structure for arr=[2,1,3,0], k=3, using pairs (value,index). After processing i=0..2 (2,1,3), what does the structure contain (front to back)?

wrongExplanations.1: We remove back values ≥ new; 2 is removed before 1 is appended.
wrongExplanations.2: The 2 is removed when 1 is added.

mcqChain.hint: Remove from back while last ≥ current before appending.

mcqChain.questionText: Continuing the previous simulation, process i=3 with value 0. What remains and what is the window minimum for window ending at i=3?
mcqChain.explanation: Pop back while ≥0: remove (3,2),(1,1); append (0,3). Front is 0.

wrongExplanations.1: Older (1,1) is removed because 1 ≥ 0.

mcqChain.hint: Remove larger/equal from back, then outdated at front if any.

mcqChain.questionText: Why must we store indices (not just values) in the candidate structure?

options.label: To know when a candidate falls out of the current window
Values are insufficient because minima require positions to compare
Indices allow faster comparison of values

mcqChain.explanation: We remove candidates whose index is before the window start; without indices we can’t detect that.

wrongExplanations.1: XOR uses values; indices don’t affect XOR directly.
wrongExplanations.3: Comparing values doesn’t need indices; window validity does.

mcqChain.hint: Out-of-window removal depends on positions.

mcqChain.questionText: When the new value equals the last candidate’s value, what comparison keeps the structure compact without affecting correctness?

options.label: Remove while last value >= new value
Remove while last value > new value
Never remove equals; only remove strictly larger

mcqChain.explanation: Removing ≥ keeps only the newest among equals; the older equal can never be needed once a newer equal exists.

wrongExplanations.1: Keeping duplicates increases size without benefit in this use case.

mcqChain.hint: If two equal values exist, which one is more useful as the window slides?

mcqChain.questionText: At which indices i (0-based) should we XOR the current minimum into the answer?
mcqChain.explanation: The first full window ends at i=k−1, and every subsequent index adds one window.

wrongExplanations.1: This would only pick every k-th window, which is incorrect.

mcqChain.hint: XOR once per full window.

mcqChain.questionText: Given current value v = x_i, how do we compute the next value v = x_{i+1}?
mcqChain.explanation: Directly apply the recurrence: x_{i+1} = (a*x_i + b) mod c.

wrongExplanations.1: The recurrence adds b; it does not subtract.
wrongExplanations.2: b is added before multiplication by a?

mcqChain.hint: Match the problem’s formula exactly.

mcqChain.questionText: Choose the correct import line to support fast input and a double-ended queue.

options.label: import sys; from collections import deque
from sys import deque; import collections
import io; from heapq import heappush, heappop

mcqChain.explanation: We need sys for input and deque from collections for the candidate structure.

wrongExplanations.1: deque is not a top-level module.
wrongExplanations.2: deque is in collections; from sys import deque is invalid.

mcqChain.hint: deque lives in the collections module.
mcqChain.codeSnippet: import sys; from collections import deque

mcqChain.questionText: Read all six integers and initialize the data structure and variables compactly.
mcqChain.explanation: Input has exactly six integers; initialize deque, result, and current value v.

wrongExplanations.1: Reading twice splits the input incorrectly and dq should be a deque, not a list.
wrongExplanations.3: dq must be constructed (deque()), and buffer.read is faster than read for large input.

mcqChain.hint: Use a single read and construct deque().

mcqChain.questionText: Set up the main loop over indices 0..n-1.
mcqChain.explanation: We need to process n elements, i from 0 to n-1.

wrongExplanations.1: Starts at 1 and misses index 0.

mcqChain.hint: Use a simple counted loop with n iterations.

mcqChain.questionText: Inside the loop, remove back candidates with value >= current v before appending.
mcqChain.explanation: We repeatedly pop from the back while last value ≥ v to keep only useful candidates.

wrongExplanations.1: Using > keeps equal duplicates unnecessarily.
wrongExplanations.3: We must consider all larger/equal back elements, not just one.

mcqChain.hint: Ensure all worse candidates are removed, not just the last one.

mcqChain.questionText: Append the current value and index as a candidate.
mcqChain.explanation: Store (value,index) at the back to maintain front as current minimum.

wrongExplanations.1: Order and type matter; we consistently use (value,index) tuples.
wrongExplanations.2: Appending to the left would break front=min invariant.

mcqChain.hint: Keep (value,index) pairs, appending at the back.

mcqChain.questionText: Remove outdated candidates from the front if they’re before the window start.
mcqChain.explanation: Indices ≤ i−k are outside [i−k+1..i]; remove one from front if so.

wrongExplanations.1: This leaves index i−k in the deque, which is outside.
wrongExplanations.2: Outdated elements are at the front, not the back.

mcqChain.hint: Check the front candidate’s index against i−k.

mcqChain.questionText: XOR the current minimum when a full window is formed, then advance the generator.
mcqChain.explanation: When i≥k−1, front holds the window minimum. Then update v for the next iteration.

wrongExplanations.1: Order doesn’t affect correctness here, but keep logic grouped for clarity.
wrongExplanations.2: Should include i=k−1, not strictly greater.

mcqChain.hint: Front element is the minimum; use XOR when we have k items.

mcqChain.questionText: Finally, print the result.
mcqChain.explanation: res is already an integer; print(res) suffices.

wrongExplanations.1: Must write a string, not an int.
wrongExplanations.3: Also correct, but the simpler print is enough.

mcqChain.hint: A simple print works.

mcqChain.questionText: Quick check: n=5, k=2, x=4, a=2, b=1, c=7. What does the program print?

wrongExplanations.1: That’s the first window minimum only.

mcqChain.hint: Combine all four minima via XOR.

mcqChain.questionText: If k = n, what does the program compute?

options.label: The XOR of a single value: the minimum of the entire sequence
The maximum of the entire sequence

mcqChain.explanation: There is exactly one window: the whole array; we XOR its minimum once.

wrongExplanations.1: That’s the k=1 case.

mcqChain.hint: Number of windows is n−k+1.

mcqChain.questionText: If c = 1, what are the generated values and how does it affect the minima?

options.label: x1=x; all subsequent values are 0; window minima quickly become 0 once a 0 enters each window
All values equal x forever; minima always x
Values increase by a each time; minima follow a ramp

mcqChain.explanation: Modulo 1 yields 0 for i≥2. After first zeros appear, every window containing a 0 has minimum 0.

wrongExplanations.1: Modulo 1 collapses to 0 regardless of a and b.

mcqChain.hint: Any integer mod 1 is 0.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the overall time complexity of the approach that maintains a candidate structure as described?
mcqChain.explanation: Each element is appended once and popped at most once from the back/front, yielding linear time.

wrongExplanations.1: We avoid scanning k for every window.
wrongExplanations.2: No sorting or log-factor operations are used.

mcqChain.hint: Amortized cost per element is constant.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the additional space used by the candidate structure at any time?
mcqChain.explanation: The structure holds at most one candidate per element in the current window.

wrongExplanations.1: We never store the entire array.
wrongExplanations.2: It can grow with window size in worst case.

mcqChain.hint: Bounded by the window size.

mcqChain.questionText: Which edge case requires careful index handling to avoid off-by-one errors?

options.label: Removing front candidates with condition index <= i - k

mcqChain.explanation: Using <= i−k correctly excludes elements outside [i−k+1..i]. Other steps are straightforward.

wrongExplanations.1: This is the primary off-by-one risk.
wrongExplanations.2: Generator update follows the formula directly.

mcqChain.hint: Compare the window start index to the front candidate index.

finalCodeSnippets: import sys; from collections import deque

reasoningChain.question: How is the array generated and how many windows are there?
reasoningChain.insight: Generate values on the fly using the recurrence and note there are n−k+1 windows.

reasoningChain.question: Why is scanning each window too slow?
reasoningChain.insight: Naive O(n·k) scanning is infeasible for n up to 1e7.

reasoningChain.question: What should we store to update minima efficiently?
reasoningChain.insight: Maintain indices of candidate elements in a double-ended structure with increasing values so the front is the minimum.

reasoningChain.question: How do we update the structure per element?
reasoningChain.insight: Before appending (v,i), remove back elements with value ≥ v; then remove front if its index ≤ i−k.

reasoningChain.question: When do we record a window minimum?
reasoningChain.insight: Once i ≥ k−1, the front holds the current window’s minimum; XOR it into the result.

reasoningChain.question: How to integrate the generator without extra memory?
reasoningChain.insight: Iteratively update v=(a*v+b)%c in the main loop; no need to store the array.
